topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt:
What is the biggest number of queens of two colors (black and white) one can place on a chessboard, such that no two of them beat one another (the queens do not beat through each other)?

Note: The queen is able to move any number of squares vertically, horizontally or diagonally.
response_text:
To find the maximum number of queens of two colors (black and white) that can be placed on an $8 \times 8$ chessboard such that no two queens of the same color attack each other, we must ensure that any two queens of the same color in the same row, column, or diagonal are separated by at least one queen of the opposite color. 

First, observe that no two queens of the same color can be adjacent horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. If they were, there would be no space between them for a queen of the opposite color to block the attack. This means that the set of white queens and the set of black queens must each form an independent set in the king's graph (a graph where edges connect squares that are a king's move apart).

An $8 \times 8$ chessboard can be partitioned into 16 disjoint $2 \times 2$ squares. In the king's graph, each $2 \times 2$ square forms a clique of 4 mutually adjacent squares. Since a queen of a given color can occupy at most one square in each $2 \times 2$ clique, there can be at most 16 white queens and 16 black queens. This gives a theoretical maximum of $16 + 16 = 32$ queens.
